Hey this is Jeff Walker and we're going to talk about niches today. Niches or niches,
depending where you are in the country. The thing I've noticed about entrepreneurs and
marketers, they tend to be very interested in markets because markets are so, so different.
They all have different characteristics and it becomes endlessly fascinating to think
about different markets. If you're selling to lawyers, or you're selling to massage
therapists, very different markets, and they're going to have lots and lots of different
characteristics. Some of that is what we're going to walk through in this bonus, and we're
really going to try to show you how to settle on a niche.
One other thing just to remember is this isn't the be all and end all. It's not like you
only get one shot at this. I started teaching people about the stock market. That was my
niche - the stock market, and investing, actually trading. Right there, there's two different
markets, investing and trading. To an outsider they seem like they're the same market,
but they're very, very different.
They both involve the stock market, but they're both very, very different from each
other. In any case, I started it in the stock market. I moved eventually, after about eight
years or so, eight or nine years, I moved to teaching people about building a business
online.
Incredibly different markets. It's just like when you get out of college, or if you get
out of college, your first job is likely not going to be your last job. Here your first niche, it's
likely not going to be your last one. You can change. You can multiple niches even. This
isn't like a life and death question, but it is important to think about a lot of things as you
focus on your first market.
First of all, there's a big question. Do you want to follow your passion, or do you
want to follow the money regardless of whether you're interested in that market or not?
Some people, a lot of people, just want to ... "I'm passionate about teaching yoga," or "I'm
passionate about golf. That's really what I want to go into. It's my life.”
That's fine, 100% fine. Even if you're in that mode, I think this recording will be
really valuable for you because there's often submarkets within a market, and it's ... you'll
cover a lot of ground. That's good for you to understand. On the other hand, there's a lot

of people that are like, "I just love building businesses," or "I love the marketing process. I
love going in and figure out markets."
In that case, you fall into that second category. There's no right or wrong answer.
You could be one or the other. I've mostly been the first one, but there's been a few times

That is a big question for you right off the bat. There are four, what we call, mega-
markets. These are just huge, huge markets. The first one is making money. The
second one is relationships. The third one is health, and the fourth one is
hobbies. Those are ... They're not only mega-markets in that they generate huge sales,
and huge dollars, and huge opportunities, but they also ... they're really big umbrellas.
Making money could be anything from how to interview for a job, to how to start a
business, to investing in real estate, to investing in the stock market. It's a huge, huge
spectrum. Same thing with relationships. It could be how to get dates, or how to raise
your kids, or how to find a marriage partner, or it could be how to build a great network of
friends.
It could be how to have a better marriage once you're already married. It's huge,
huge, huge, huge market. Health, incredibly big market. It could be anything from body
building, lifting weights, to fighting cancer, to yoga, to all the different types of treating
different diseases, staying healthy, eating healthy, meditation. It could be just a huge,
huge umbrella there.
Hobbies, it could be like hobbies or sports. It could be scuba diving, or tennis, or
golf, or stamp collecting, or dressage, or cake decorating, or knitting. Huge, huge area
there. Now one of the things here is that they can overlap. You could have ... You could
have a niece that's teaching people how to walk dogs as a profession. That combines dogs
and money.
Often there's lots and lots of markets that overlay in one of these mega-markets
especially those first three on top of it could really be a great niche. You could have
something for truck drivers, which is about making money, about for them how to stay
healthy, because they're in their truck driving all day long. What are exercises you can do
as you're driving your truck?
How can you eat healthy when you're driving across the country and stopping in

truck stops all the time? I just want to put these out there because you definitely want to

There are all kinds of different ways that you can layer these mega-markets so

Approach number one is to focus on you.
This is where you look at what are your core passions and interests? What skills
have you developed? What knowledge have you acquired? What challenges have you
overcome? What would other people say that you do really well? What do people come to
you to ask for advice? It's amazing because I remember when I was starting out and I first
learned about publishing online, and creating information or wisdom-based products.
At that time, I was studying non-stop about the stock market because I was just
fascinated with it. I was just study, study, study, study on the market. I was also a stay-at-
home dad, taking care of a couple of little babies at the time. I had a very full day taking
care of these two kids.
Every waking minute, if I could get them down for a nap I would be studying the
stock market, I'd be trying to build these computer models, these newer network-based
models, artificial intelligence based models that would ... where you could predict where
the stock market was going so I was obsessed with it.
Then my wife would come home, we'd get the kids in bed, and then I would just
study until all hours of the night and into the early morning, study the stock market. Then
finally I would lay down in bed, and as I'm laying down in bed, I would, for a couple
minutes before I fell asleep, I would think about, "Oh man, there's that information
market. It would be such a great business. What can I publish about?"
Then I would get up the next day, and I'd spend the day taking care of the kids, and
every spare minute, studying the stock market, studying the stock market, studying the
stock market. Looking at data that went back 100 years in the stock market. Then I
exhausted. I would fall into bed and say, "Boy, what can I publish about? What can I
publish ...?"

This went on for weeks, and months. All of a sudden one day, when I got really

That's where you look at these types of questions, especially what do other people
think you do really well? What do other people ... What do you do that other people find
fascinating? That's approach number one.
Approach number two is to focus on your avatar. This might be a case
where you sort of have this general, vague idea of what direction you're going in, and you
can think about who's your typical client, your perfect client?
That's what the word avatar means. It's just like a fictional representation of who
your perfect client is. If you start to think about who that client is, or who'd you want to
work with, or who you want to help, then you start to think, "What do they come to you
for help with? What do they ask you questions about?"
Maybe you have existing clients that you work with. What do they struggle with?
What keeps them up at night? What are their pain points? What are their challenges?
What are the common questions they always come to you with? What are the common
questions that they ask on blogs or in the comment section in other programs?
What about the best-selling books on your topic, whatever your topic is, there's
likely a bunch of books, and if there aren't, it's probably not a good market. We'll talk
about that later on, but go to Amazon. Type in your ... like the keywords for your topic,
and look and see what are the top-ranked books? On Amazon you can see what all books
are ranked in terms of their sales rank. You scroll down and it tells you how they're
ranked.
Go into forums on your topic. Just whatever your topic is, like let's say it's guitar.
You could type in ... Go to Google, and type in guitar, space, and then forums, and you can
find the top forums. You can Google those forums and see what are people asking?
Often in those forums, they'll have sticky topics, which are up at ... always stay up
at the top of the forum. They'll be like frequently asked questions. You can look in there,
or just look in the forums and look for the most popular topics that have hundreds or
thousands of views and replies, and look at those and see what those topics are. You can

start to figure out what people are really struggling with, what they're interested in.
Then, if by some crazy notion, you have an email list, you can send an email and
you can ask people what they're struggling with. If you could sit down with me for coffee,
what would be the number one question you would ask me? That's a great way to ask your
list what they're struggling with.
Approach number three is to follow the money. This is where you find out
who's making money and see how you can do it differently or do it better. What
improvements you can make, or could you just bring your own particular voice to it?
Can you bring your own personality to it? Often, in this case, one of the things ... If
you're thinking about going into a market, you could just take a look and see who's
advertising in that market? Either in magazines, or on Google, or on Facebook. It will tell
you ... If people are advertising, especially if they're advertising they've been around for a
while as advertisers.
Go look at magazines and look at the given magazine on a topic. Maybe it's scuba
diving. Maybe it's golf, or maybe it's whatever. Look back a year, and if there's ads that
were running a year ago that are still running, they're paying a lot of money for those ads.
That will tell you something that is working in that market.
Then there's this idea of Evergreen markets versus seasonal markets or fad
markets. You want to look at a market that's been around, and that's going to be around.
Ryan Levesque was just telling me about when he started out. One of the markets he was
in was making jewelry out of Scrabble tiles, the game Scrabble.
It was a nice market. It was a hot market for a little while, but it was sort of a fad
that went out. You don't want to build your business on a fad. There are seasonal markets
like skiing is a seasonal market, or Christmas, or Halloween.
If you built your business around Halloween, then you're putting all your eggs in a
basket and you're going to be busy for about two months of the year, or one month of the
year, and the rest of the year you're going to be wondering what to do.
If something goes wrong in that one Halloween, you could be out of business. If
you're going to Evergreen markets like that, then you probably want to have them offset.
For instance, here in my hometown here in Durango, Colorado, there's a lot of sports
enthusiasts who like to ski, and like to mountain bike.

One of the shops in town, actually a couple of the shops in town, they specialize in
skiing in the winter, in the fall and winter, and mountain biking in the spring and
summer. Those seasonal markets offset each other. You either want seasonal markets that
offset each other, or probably better to go with Evergreen markets.
In terms of following the money, you want to look for markets with multiple types
of products at different price points. It's ideal if you can find a market that does support
higher price points. One of the things you can do is go to Clickbank and you can search in
their marketplace by niches.
You can find just all kinds of products there. You can actually tell, if you go in there
as an affiliate, and look at in your marketplace as an affiliate, then you can look at the
gravity scores. They will tell you how well that product is selling. It's great. You can
actually look at all these different information-based products and see what's selling.
You always want to remember those big four proven markets, and combine them,
or niche them down. Like I said, the making money market, that's not really a market.
There's this big umbrella. What I went into at first, there's all kinds of different, as I
mentioned, different sub-niches there and one of the big ones is the stock market, and
investing.
Let's just say investing is a really big one. Then under investing there's investing in
all kinds of different things including the financial markets. From there, within the
financial markets, there's all kinds ... there's currency, there's commodities and futures,
there's the stock market, there's bonds, there's options.
I'm sure I'm forgetting a whole bunch. Gold, so there's a whole bunch of them
there. When I started publishing there, and I really didn't know anything, this is just sort
of ... I had this real narrow niche interest so I started that niche. I was publishing for
short-term stock index future traders. That's like a little, tiny niche of a little, tiny niche.
It's actually gotten a lot bigger now. It was much, much smaller back then. It was
actually just starting out back then. That's an example of within a big, umbrella market,
niching down, and then niching down, and then niching down, and then niching down
within that. That's what I did. If you're in it for those mega-markets, you've got to do
something like that.
Susan Garrett is one of my students. Incredibly successful. She teaches dog agility

training market, then there's the competitive dog training market, then there's the dog

In terms of researching and testing a market, you want to absolutely ... First thing I
do is Google the niche and see what comes up. When it comes up, you want to look for
who's advertising, and you also want to look for who's at the top in terms of what we call
the natural search. The people that are at the top of the Google ranks that didn't pay for
the Google rankings.
The top few rankings are always going to have ... they'll say ad under them, and
those are the people that are advertising. You want to click through and follow what
they're doing. Look at what products they're selling. Focus on whether they're building a
list, if they're building a list. How are they building it? What is their lead magnet that
they're giving away? What are they publishing?
I would go get a second email address and subscribe to all the top advertisers. I'd
go probably the top 10 advertisers, 10 to 20 advertisers, and the top 20, to 30, to 40 top
sites. I would subscribe to everything. That's why I said have it go to a second email
address. Then I would put together a spread sheet. I would list them in order by
how they're appearing.
The top sites that were advertising, and also the top sites in the natural search. I
would put name of the site, the URL, if they're building a list or not, what
they're selling, what kind of price points they're selling at. Then I would start
to follow their emails. Watch the emails coming in, and start to judge how good their
marketing is.
Then I would look to see if they're ... what they're selling. If they're selling other
things, other than their own products, whose other products are they selling? I would look
to see if they're starting to promote. If you've got a list now of the top 30, or 40, or 50
sites, are they promoting for each other? Are there joint venture deals going back and
forth?
The big question is are they selling in this market? One of the things in there that
says if there's no competition, I'd be very careful. You don't want to go into a market
where there's no one on there. You've got this brand ... You came up with this new idea.
No one's ever thought of this before. It's going to be amazing.

That's generally a warning sign. The odds are you aren't the first person in the

the products.
I love being in a market that people are passionate about, that they're rabid about.
People are just ... There's some markets where people just get just really into it. Golf is a
market. Scuba diving is a market. Dog training is a market. Pets in general. Relationships.
Finding dates. For me, people are ... I teach how to build a business. I'm teaching how to

People are super passionate about that because of what it's going to bring to them

that's an example where you would niche out. If you're selling race tires for rally cars, or
for autocross cars, people are into that in a big way.
That would work. Just general tires for Camrys, probably not. People aren't so

blues.
For guitar collectors, for people that are into historic guitars, for people who are

It's like that if you go to a good periodical store you can see all kinds of magazines
you never thought of. That will give you ideas for niches. You can go to amazon.com,

You can look and see if there's a trade association for the market. I've never really

You can look for the cost-per-click. If you go ... What you should do is you should
join Google Adwords. That is Google's advertising platform. Anyone can join. It's just
adwords.google.com or just do a search on Google for Google Adwords and you can go
there and you can join up. It costs nothing, and you get all kinds of information there
including you can see how much people are paying for a click in for different key words.
If people are paying like $4 or $5 for a click, that means it's a very, very valuable
key word. There's some where people are paying a lot more than that for a click. One
thing you can do is if you're narrowed down, you say, "I'm interested in this market, you
can go back to your spread sheet where you've got all those top sites, and try to, just by
looking around and seeing who seems to be doing well? Who's up at the top of the ads?
Who's doing joint ventures with each other, so I get a feeling for some of the top products
that are selling.
You can go ahead and see if they've got an affiliate program, you can join the
affiliate program and you can watch how they work with their affiliates. Watch the emails
they send to their affiliates. Watch that affiliate process. You can even go crazy and you
could go and buy some advertising and send some traffic into that affiliate program and
see how well it's converted.
Certainly one thing you want to be looking at as you're doing all this research and
testing, is you want to be thinking about what possible offers you can make, and analyze
those in terms of the demand, in terms of the competition, in terms of what quality is
really required within the market.
What are other people doing? Part of this ... It would be best if you went out and
bought some of the programs from your would-be competitors, and start to understand
what they're doing, how they're doing it. Watch their sales process. See what is the
possible hot points, because just by watching their sales process, you can figure out what
they've learned about the market.

You can look at what kind of upsells they have. What kind of downsells they have.
You can look at what type of quality, how high of quality are they? Is there marketing?
How high of quality is their actual product? If they're doing launches, take a look at their

You can start to understand what do you have to do to compete in this market?
Then you can think about, based on that quality, what's going to be your speed to market?
How quickly can you create something to compete in that market? Again, I think in this

That's another thing to look at in the market. Do people tend to promote for each

to build a list, and become one of their more significant partners by making sales for
them. Then generally they'll turn around and promote for you.
Part of your research and your testing is to look for the different factors of demand.

It all really depends on the market and sort of the pricing within the market, but I

What kind of competition is there? Are people in that market buying online?

You're looking for competitors who have been there for a while, who are making

You can look and see if you're getting conversions. Basically it'll be an opt-in page

I didn't actually sell, I put up an opt-in page and it was like put in your email

There's things that I want you to avoid here. As you're thinking about market, and
as you're thinking about niche, you need to avoid.
One of them is thinking, "I'm not enough of an expert on whatever the topic is."
The reality is you don't need to be the greatest expert in the world because for instance
one of the things you can do is you can leverage the knowledge of other people who are
experts. Generally, there are a ton of people that would love to be interviewed by you.
It's amazing how easy it is to get experts to agree to an interview. All of them are
starving for more attention. You might not get the top echelon, the top expert of all
experts, but you can still get an expert to do an interview. If you look at all the podcasts
that are happening right now, it seems like 80% of them are people just interviewing each
other.
You don't even need to interview them to pull their expertise because so many of
them are publishing on blogs, or publishing on YouTube, or publishing books, and you
can take their knowledge there and giving them full credit, but say, "Boy, I just saw this

video by so-and-so, and they are really brilliant. Here's five of the key points that they

You can do that all day long. You could do that with podcasts. You can do that with
books. You can do that with what people are blogging about. You can aggregate the
information of several different experts. Aggregate that information. Pull it together.
The thing is- in any market, most people are beginners. That's just the nature of
learning. Everyone starts as a beginner. If you look at guitar players, 90% of them are
beginners, just because everyone starts as a beginner. Eric Clapton started as a beginner.
If you look at surfers, 90% of them are beginners. Even Kelly Slater is a beginner. If
you look at golfers, 90% of them are beginners because everyone starts as a beginner. In
whatever market you're in, most of the people are going to be beginners. You only have to
... You don't have to be the greatest expert in the world. You only have to be a few steps
ahead of them.
There's a saying, "In the land of the blind, the one-eyed man is king. In the land of
the blind, the one-eyed man is king." What that's saying is you don't have to be the
world's expert. You just have to be a few steps ahead of the beginners, and then you can
teach them.
There's a flip side to this, because there's this phrase of, "Fake it 'til you make it."
There are times where that's a really scary, scary thing. If you're in a situation where
you're teaching someone something that they could get in trouble with, like serious
trouble, or danger, or they might lose their life savings.
When I was teaching about the stock market, I didn't have any credentials. I didn't
have a ton of experience as a trader, but I was working with someone. I had a mentor who
did have a lot of experience. He'd been trading successfully for a decade. Basically what I
was doing was we were partners and I was publishing his work.
Oh by the way, I'd spent years studying the market. I knew, just myself, I knew so
much more than almost anyone. Enough where I could give good, solid advice, plus I had
the work of my partner who had been trading for a living for 10 years. If you're going to
teach someone about parenting, and you're not a parent, you don't want to mess kids up.
You don't want to mess parents up, so you've got to be careful. I'm just, in general, not a
fan of a fake it 'til you make it. I am a fan of becoming an expert or bringing other experts
out there, and then leading people forward.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

As you're thinking about this, some of the questions to ask is even if you aren't the
world's greatest expert, can you help them take action? Can you help them stay
accountable? If you're teaching on a topic, you don't have to be the greatest expert on that
topic to inspire them, to get them to move. Even beginners, slightly advanced beginners,
who are starting to have some success, to be tremendously inspirational to other
beginners, to get them to take action.
You could help them stay accountable. You can get them moving towards real
results. Those things really could be way more important than having the status as an
incredible expert.
One of my most successful Product Launch Formula owners is John Gallagher.
John teaches about edible medicinal plants and herbs. It's a topic that he's been studying,
and his wife have been studying for years, and years, and years. Really, John will tell you,
"I'm not the expert. I bring the experts to the market." He has ... even though he's got a lot
of expertise, he's actually become really, just really great at the marketing, at the business
side. He finds other world-class experts, and he helps them create courses and bring them
out to the world.
Another big thing to avoid is following a passion of yours without checking out the
market, without really testing the market, without understanding the market. Big part of
this I see happen a lot is people offer what they think the market wants, and not what they
actually want, or even worse, is offering what you think the market needs, and not what it
actually want.
There's this old saying that, "It's easier to sell the cure than it is to sell prevention."
Many people, especially in markets they're really passionate about, if you really know the
inside of the market, and you really know what's going to move the needle, then you really
know what they need, but it's hard to sell what they need.
Going back to guitar, I have someone that I follow and they teach the super ... They
teach how to practice correctly, which is the key to all playing. Some of the stuff they
teach is like ... It's moving your fingers so slowly. Like literally it might take five seconds
to move one finger from one string to the next string. Like five seconds. Like one second,
two seconds, three seconds, four seconds, five seconds to move one finger from one string
to another. It's the absolute most core fundamentals that you need. This is like how to
really learn true mastery of the guitar. However, and I think this person who teaches this
does an incredible job, and I have followed this person's work.

This person doesn't have a very big business because most people, even though

They want to learn from someone that's going to teach them super-fast. That's what

The reality is, this is an advanced concept, and I'm going to get into this throughout

the market wants. You sell them what they want, and you give them what they need. You
sell them, "We're going to teach you how to play songs really quick."
Once they get the program, then you have to figure out a way to give them what
they need, which is being super fundamental in how they move their fingers on the string,

those songs really quickly.
That's your challenge is to give them what they want, and then if what they want

going to truly move them forward, but first you have to sell them what they want.
Again, this is super advanced. I went through it pretty quick. We will get to this

You definitely want to do your research that we covered in this training. You want to get
... One of the keys is trying to get as much feedback as you develop your product.
When I teach Product Launch Formula, I teach how to do the seed launch. The seed
launch is a super interactive process where you're getting feedback every step of the way
because that feedback will help you create a great product, and will also help you figure
out what the objections are, and what the market wants. It will not only inform the actual
product ... The feedback will not only inform the product, but will also inform your
marketing as well.
Another big thing to avoid is not niching down enough. You really do need to niche
down. You want to avoid going super broad and wide. You want to be narrow and deep in
your topic. If you try to serve everyone, you're going to serve no one.

Here's an example of niching down. You might have an interest in ADD okay, but
that's a huge market. You might want to focus on parents of children with ADD, but that's
pretty big too, so you might want to focus down on parents of elementary kids who are
ADD.
You might even want to go tighter than that, or at least there might be that your
market might be for parents of elementary kids with ADD, but then a product might be
for parents of elementary ... of children with ... elementary aged kids, but solving their
discipline problems, so there you've taken it down, you've niched down to a market, and
then when you're actually going down to a product, it might be actually focusing on
discipline problems within those elementary aged ADD kids.
That's an example of niching down. Finally, just to wrap this up, you see now we're
getting pretty long here. Don't overcomplicate this. We've got a guide for you here as a
download, and it's basically actually an action guide in finding your niche.
Remember, you're not going to 100% know everything and get this perfectly right
the first time you try it. It is about baby steps. It's about gradually, it doesn't have to be
slowly, but gradually moving forward in your knowledge of your market, your knowledge
of the characteristics of the market, of who else is out in the market, and gradually
tightening down.
Remember you might need to pivot. In other words, you're starting in one
direction, you might need to move into a different direction. A slightly different direction,
or a big change. Finally remember this isn't the be all, end all. There's very, very few
people that start off in one place, and stay in that market for the rest of their lives.
Almost everyone, at some point, shifts markets. It could be a massive shift like I
made, or it could be a minor shift, but this isn't you're doing it forever. One decision and
it's got to be the perfect decision. I hope that helped you with getting some ideas on your
niche, and sort of starting to narrow down on what direction you want to start in.
